Final Project

Hima Shanthi Rayapati, Khanh Bui, Serena Sabbara

2024-05-02

Load the libraries

## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ purrr     1.0.2
## ✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.1
## ✔ ggplot2   3.4.4     ✔ tibble    3.2.1
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.3     ✔ tidyr     1.3.0
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

What is ANCOVA?

Analysis of covariance (ANCOVA):

A statistical technique used to compare means of two or more groups while accounting for the influence of other variables, called covariates.

Linear model that tests the influence of

  • One categorical explanatory variable and

  • One continuous explanatory variable

  • One continuous response variable.

Why ANCOVA?

Detect a difference in means of 2 or more independent groups, whilst controlling for scale covariates.

  • A covariate is not usually part of the main research question but could influence the dependent variable and therefore needs to be controlled for.

Help addressing several key challenges and providing valuable insights

  • Controlling for Covariates

  • Improving Precision and Accuracy

  • Addressing Confounding Variables

Possible goals of ANCOVA

Determine the effects of the categorical and continuous variables on the response variable

  1. The categorical and continuous variables on the response variable

  2. The categorical variable(s) on the response variable(s) after removing the effect of the continuous variable

  3. The categorical variable(s) on the relationship between the continuous variables(s) and the response variable

Note: these goals are only met if there is no significant interaction between the categorical and continuous variables!

  • In case of significant interaction, the scenario will look like graph 1 and 2

ANCOVA and ANOVA differences

Flowchart

Our research question

  • Is the time difference between working before and during the pandemic influenced by occupation? (covary for age)

Variables of Interest

Numeric response variables: time_bp (time spent on work before the pandemic) and time_dp (time spent on work during a pandemic)

  • We looked at the difference between time_bp and time_dp (time_bp - time_dp)

Categorical explanatory variable: Occupation

Numeric covariate: Age

Load the data

Hurdle

  • We got an error while trying to copy the path

  • Later changed it to:

Psychological Effects of COVID

  • Survey taken in 2020 to understand people’s opinion on lockdown
## Rows: 1,175
## Columns: 22
## $ age            <chr> "19-25", "Dec-18", "19-25", "19-25", "19-25", "19-25", …
## $ gender         <chr> "Male", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Male", "Male…
## $ occupation     <chr> "Student in College", "Student in School", "Student in …
## $ line_of_work   <chr> "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "",…
## $ time_bp        <int> 7, 7, 7, 7, 7, 5, 5, 7, 5, 7, 7, 5, 7, 7, 9, 11, 7, 7, …
## $ time_dp        <int> 5, 11, 7, 7, 7, 7, 4, 9, 9, 7, 4, 9, 11, 7, 11, 5, 5, 9…
## $ travel_time    <dbl> 0.5, 0.5, 1.5, 1.5, 1.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 1.5, 0.5, …
## $ easeof_online  <int> 3, 4, 2, 3, 2, 4, 1, 3, 1, 2, 2, 3, 5, 2, 2, 5, 2, 5, 2…
## $ home_env       <int> 3, 2, 2, 1, 2, 4, 3, 2, 2, 2, 1, 3, 4, 1, 3, 2, 3, 3, 4…
## $ prod_inc       <dbl> 0.0, -0.5, 1.0, 0.0, 0.0, -1.0, 0.5, 0.0, 1.0, 0.0, 0.0…
## $ sleep_bal      <dbl> 0.0, 0.5, 0.0, 1.0, 0.0, -1.0, 0.5, -0.5, 1.0, 0.0, -0.…
## $ new_skill      <dbl> 0.5, -1.0, 0.5, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0, 1.0, -0.5, 1.0, 0.0, 0.5…
## $ fam_connect    <dbl> 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0, -0.5, 0.5, -1.0, 1.0, -1.0, 0.…
## $ relaxed        <dbl> -0.5, 1.0, 0.5, -1.0, 0.5, 0.0, 1.0, -0.5, 1.0, 0.0, 0.…
## $ self_time      <dbl> -0.5, 1.0, 0.5, -0.5, 0.0, 0.0, 1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 0.0, 0.5…
## $ like_hw        <int> 100, 1111, 1100, 100, 1010, 110, 1100, 1000, 1010, 1110…
## $ dislike_hw     <int> 1, 1110, 111, 1111, 1000, 1100, 1101, 1000, 100, 1111, …
## $ prefer         <chr> "Complete Physical Attendance", "Complete Physical Atte…
## $ certaindays_hw <chr> "Yes", "No", "Yes", "Yes", "Yes", "No", "Yes", "Yes", "…
## $ X              <lgl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA,…

## Working Professional:Dec-18-Student in School:Dec-18                                                                          NA

Results

  • Significant effects of the explanatory variable (occupation; multiple levels), continuous numerical covariate (age), and the interaction on the response variable (Diff_in_time)

  • Occupation: p < .0001

  • Age: p < .0001

  • Occupation:Age : p < .0001

Note: In R, the p-value was so low that the output indicated ***, which means p = 0.

Discussion

  • There was a statistically significant one-way interaction between occupation and the difference in time spent working (before and after the pandemic), whilst controlling for age, p = 0.

  • A post-hoc analysis (TukeyHSD) compared all of the groups, which resulted in a very long output, but this was done to determine simple main effects.

Note: This is important, but our project focused on learning ANCOVA, so we will not go into detail about the main effects/post-hoc analysis.

Summary

ANCOVA

  • Very similar to ANOVA but controls for random noise

  • Different assumptions than ANOVA

  • R syntax is not super difficult!

    • Can use lm() or aov()/anova() functions

    • TukeyHSD for post-hoc analyses

Project

  • We achieved our goal of learning how to use ANCOVA in R

  • Future directions would include an easier way of doing a simple main effects analysis with so many levels of the categorical variable/factor

Code for including pictures/images

References

  • Data Collected from Kaggle :

    • https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/hemanthhari/psycological-effects-of-covid

  • Links used to learn about ANCOVA and slidy:

    • https://r.qcbs.ca/workshop04/book-en/analysis-of-covariance-ancova.html#running-an-ancova

    • https://testbook.com/key-differences/difference-between-anova-and-ancova

    • https://dzchilds.github.io/stats-for-bio/introduction-to-ancova.html

    • https://youtu.be/sFBJbKHHAIo?feature=shared
